summary: Hungary's rising special-educational-needs (SEN/SNI/BTMN) identification—registered numbers have climbed roughly 40% over twenty years, reaching 112,060–112,007 by 2024/25 with sharp acceleration after 2021/22 [F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-01][F-live-legal_and_governance-01][F-live-hungarian_education_system-01]—presents six distinguishable transformation paths, none a clear winner. A passive baseline (T1) treats current drift as a counterfactual: it changes nothing in gatekeeping, category definitions, or finance, but leaves demographic contraction (0-4 population down 5% by 2033 [F-live-demography-02], births falling 22% since 2021 [F-live-demography-01]) interacting with a static, aging, unevenly distributed workforce [F-live-demography-06], unresolved ECtHR/EU obligations [F-live-legal_and_governance-09][F-live-political_feasibility-06][F-live-political_feasibility-09], and county disparities (5.4%-14% [F-live-hungarian_education_system-02]) to continue unaddressed—most lenses judge this not fiscally, legally, or equity-neutral even though it requires no new coalition.　　Measurement reform (T2) is the lowest-risk, foundational option: it would reconcile divergent series (92,800→97,300 vs 112,060 vs 69,000 [F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-01][F-live-school_network_planning-01]) and decompose true prevalence from diagnostic-practice, definitional, and incentive effects, using natural experiments like the 2018 BTMN grading-exemption change [F-live-implementation_planning-02] and the 2020 school-readiness rule tightening, which is directly linked to accelerated SNI growth [F-live-hungarian_education_system-08][F-live-school_network_planning-04]. Comparative and Finnish evidence supports this decomposition logic strongly (OECD attributes most autism-diagnosis growth to detection improvements [F-live-demography-05][F-live-international_comparison-03]; Finland's post-2010 growth concentrated in one new tier while the top tier stayed flat [F-live-finnish_reform-02]). Its limitation: better data does not itself change access or resource allocation.　　Structural/categorical redesign (T3) targets the sole nationwide expert-committee gatekeeper and its documented multi-month waiting lists [F-live-hungarian_education_system-04], drawing on Wales' non-categorical ALN transition (11.2% vs England 13.6%, Scotland 37% [F-live-international_comparison-06]) and Finland's tiered model [F-live-international_comparison-07]. But Finland's persistent 4%-13% municipal variation after reform [F-live-finnish_reform-04] and its dependence on dual-teacher/SET infrastructure Hungary lacks [F-live-international_comparison-10] limit transferability, and Poland's rapid, non-consensual 2016-2017 restructuring produced severe disruption (3,173 Warsaw students without placement, an 80%-school strike [F-live-polish_reform-07][F-live-polish_reform-09]) and even rolled back mainstreaming [F-live-polish_reform-02].　　Finance redesign (T4) would shift per-pupil multipliers (2x-3x for SNI [F-live-education_finance-08][F-live-legal_and_governance-07]) toward census-based allocation, but no causal evidence ties Hungary's specific financing mechanism to its identification rise, and Poland's contrasting expansion of disability-weighted funding (weights 1.4-9.5, subventions rising 14% independent of prevalence [F-live-polish_reform-03][F-live-polish_reform-04]) while separately centralizing gatekeeping [F-live-polish_reform-05] weakens the universality of the incentive theory, though Finland's census-shift did coincide with reduced full-time placement [F-live-finnish_reform-03].　　Workforce expansion (T5) is the most politically uncontroversial and directly evidenced option, targeting acute regional shortages (Budapest's 45 unfilled posts versus 2-3 in rural counties [F-live-hungarian_education_system-05][F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-05], districts with zero specialists [F-live-legal_and_governance-08]), but rising training enrollment does not reliably convert to practicing supply [F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-05][F-live-education_finance-07], and general salary reform (21.2% raise [F-live-portuguese_reform-03]) may not reach specialist shortages specifically.　　Anti-segregation/adjacent-policy coordination (T6) most directly confronts free school choice and selective gimnázium tracks left unaddressed by the 2023 law [F-live-hungarian_education_system-06], responding to unresolved Horváth and Kiss and Szolcsán judgments and the 2016 EU infringement procedure [F-live-hungarian_education_system-07][F-live-political_feasibility-06][F-live-political_feasibility-09], and Kertesi-Kézdi evidence that school-switching independently reshapes ethnic composition [F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-08]. It faces the highest political resistance of all six options, given a decade of documented non-compliance [F-live-political_feasibility-10] and contested NGO estimates of segregation's scale [F-live-hungarian_education_system-09].　　Decision-makers must choose between empirically weak but politically easy levers (T1, T5) and evidence-richer but disruptive or resistance-heavy structural levers (T3, T6), while T2 and T4 sit as enabling or contested intermediate steps. The research most likely to reshape this choice concerns the causal decomposition of identification drivers, the fiscal-incentive test for Hungary specifically, and whether tiered reform would relabel rather than newly serve need.
